planting.note: Plant in partial shade to deep shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 9–11; Byfield Fern (Bowenia spectabilis) is a small, unusual cycad endemic to the rainforests of central Queensland, Australia — unique among cycads for having bipinnate (twice-divided) fronds that give it a distinctly fern-like appearance — growing from a subterranean stem and producing a low, spreading crown of graceful, glossy, deeply divided fronds in the shade.

directions: Plant Bowenia spectabilis in humus-rich, moist, freely draining, slightly acidic soil in partial to deep shade; protect from frost, direct sun, and poorly drained soil — the underground stem rots readily in waterlogged conditions; fertilize lightly in spring with a slow-release cycad formulation containing trace elements. Caution: all parts are highly toxic — seeds, leaves, and stems contain harmful cycasin glycosides — keep away from children, pets, and livestock. Byfield Fern is botanically extraordinary — one of only two species in the Bowenia genus, and the only cycad genus with bipinnate fronds, where the leaflets are themselves further divided, creating a lacy, fern-like texture that is quite unlike the stiff, pinnate fronds typical of all other cycads. The plant grows from a fully subterranean, tuberous stem that barely shows above ground, producing fronds directly from the underground caudex; mature plants slowly produce offset stems underground. In its native Queensland rainforest understory, it grows in deep shade on well-drained slopes, perfectly suited to cultivation under the canopy of large trees in frost-free subtropical gardens. A rare and coveted plant in specialist collections for its uniquely fine-textured, graceful foliage and compact size.

subcategory: South African monotypic endangered shade cycad

planting.note: Plant in partial shade to bright shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in zone 9–11; Natal Grass Cycad (Stangeria eriopus) is a remarkable, endangered South African cycad — the only member of its family, Stangeriaceae — native to coastal forest margins and grasslands of KwaZulu-Natal and Eastern Cape, with fronds so similar to ferns that 19th-century botanists initially described it as a new fern genus before cycad cones were discovered.

directions: Plant Stangeria eriopus in well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; protect from frost (damaged below 28°F), direct sun (which bleaches fronds), and waterlogged conditions; apply a cycad-specific slow-release fertilizer with trace elements in spring. Caution: all parts are toxic if ingested — contain cycasin and other cycad toxins. Natal Grass Cycad is botanically exceptional — the sole species in the family Stangeriaceae, occupying an isolated position in cycad evolution with no close living relatives. It was first described by European botanists in 1853 as a fern (placed in the genus Lomaria) because its soft-textured, pinnate, fern-like fronds are completely unlike the stiff, tough fronds of other cycads — only the discovery of its cone structures confirmed its true identity. Two growth forms exist in the wild: a forest form from shaded coastal forest understory with longer, drooping, dark-green fronds, and a grassland form with shorter, stiffer, sun-adapted fronds. The species is listed as Endangered and is CITES Appendix II listed; over-collection from wild plants for use in traditional Zulu medicine (zulu: isigqiki-somkhovu — 'dwarf's stool') has severely depleted natural populations. Always buy from reputable nurseries propagating from seed.

subcategory: East Asian compact edible mulberry shrub-tree

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock after last frost in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 5–9; Korean Mulberry (Morus australis) is a compact, multi-stemmed mulberry native to Korea, Japan, China, and the Himalayas — smaller than White or Red Mulberry — bearing abundant small to medium, very sweet, juicy black-purple mulberries relished by birds and people, and with a long history of use in East Asian traditional medicine.

directions: Plant Morus australis in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; water regularly during establishment, then reduce as the plant is drought-tolerant once established; prune in winter to shape and control size; harvest berries as they ripen and turn deep purple-black, as they stain readily. Korean Mulberry is the smallest-growing of the commonly cultivated mulberry species — typically maturing as a large multi-stemmed shrub or small tree 5–15 feet tall — making it better suited to smaller gardens than the more vigorous White Mulberry (Morus alba) or American Red Mulberry (Morus rubra). The abundant small to medium berries are very sweet and richly flavored, ripening over several weeks in early to midsummer; birds are strongly attracted to fruiting trees so netting may be needed for a full harvest. In East Asian traditional medicine systems, the berries (sang shen), leaves (sang ye), bark (sang bai pi), and twigs (sang zhi) are all used in herbal preparations for treating conditions ranging from hypertension and diabetes to tinnitus and gray hair. The leaves are used for silkworm (Bombyx mori) cultivation like other mulberry species. Compact enough for container growing in large pots; prune annually to maintain size and maximize fruiting wood.
sources: Korean Rural Development Administration — Morus cultivation

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in zone 8–11; Cherry of the Rio Grande is an attractive small fruiting tree native to southern Brazil and Uruguay — popular in South Texas and Gulf Coast gardens for its profuse crops of small, sweet-tart, deep purple-red fruits in spring and early summer, its tolerance of alkaline soils, and its handsome, semi-evergreen foliage.

directions: Plant Eugenia involucrata in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; once established it tolerates moderate drought, alkaline soils, and light frost to about 25°F; harvest fruits as they deepen to dark red-purple and become fully soft, as they drop quickly when ripe. Cherry of the Rio Grande is one of the most rewarding subtropical fruiting plants for Gulf Coast and South Texas gardens — it produces an early-season crop (April–June) of small, round to pear-shaped, dark red to almost black fruits on a handsome, densely branching shrub or small tree. The fruits have a sweet, rich, aromatic flavor with a pleasant cherry-like tang; they are eaten fresh, made into jams and jellies, or fermented into wine. A close relative of the more commonly known Pitanga (Surinam Cherry, Eugenia uniflora), but with a more refined, less resinous flavor. Hardy to about 25°F (zone 8 with some protection in zone 8a), and remarkably adaptable to the alkaline, often caliche soils of South Texas where many Eugenia species fail. Plants fruit at a young age and can bear two crops per year in warm climates. Prune lightly after fruiting to maintain shape; the dense branching also makes it suitable as an informal hedge or screen.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — subtropical fruits for Texas
Rare Fruit Council International — Eugenia involucrata

planting.note: Direct sow seeds after last frost in full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in zone 9–12, or start indoors 6–8 weeks earlier; Candle Bush (Ringworm Cassia) is a large, fast-growing, pantropical medicinal shrub bearing spectacular, upright, candle-like flower buds of tightly enclosed golden-yellow blooms arranged in bold spikes — the individual flowers remaining wrapped in copper-toned bracts that peel back from the base upward as the plant blooms, creating a glowing effect — and widely used in traditional medicine across the tropics for treating skin fungal infections.

directions: Direct sow Senna alata seeds (scarify or nick seed coat before sowing to improve germination) in full sun in fertile, moist but well-drained soil; plants grow very rapidly in warm conditions and can reach 4–8 feet in a single season; deadhead spent spikes to prolong blooming and cut back hard in late winter to prevent woodiness. Candle Bush is one of the most dramatic flowering shrubs of the tropics and subtropics — its upright, glowing flower spikes are truly candle-like before the bright-yellow, butterfly-shaped individual flowers open, giving the plant an almost magical quality in the garden. It is a pan-tropical species now naturalized throughout the tropics worldwide, spread from its likely South American origin. In traditional medicine across Africa, Asia, and Latin America, a leaf paste or extract is applied topically to treat ringworm, athlete's foot, and other fungal skin infections — giving rise to the common name 'Ringworm Bush'; the antifungal compound chrysophanol has been isolated from the leaves. The plant is also an important butterfly host plant, particularly for sulphur butterflies (Catopsilia spp.) whose caterpillars feed on the leaves. In zone 9, it self-sows readily and may re-establish from seed each year after frost kills the tops; in zone 10–12, it grows as a woody perennial shrub.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Senna alata
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Senna alata

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in zone 8–11; Tea Tree (Melaleuca alternifolia) is a small Australian tree or large shrub native to the coastal swamps and creek margins of northeastern New South Wales — the source of tea tree essential oil, one of the most widely used natural antimicrobial and antiseptic agents in the world — bearing narrow, needle-like leaves rich in terpinen-4-ol and producing creamy-white bottlebrush flowers beloved by pollinators.

directions: Plant Melaleuca alternifolia in full sun to light shade in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to occasionally wet soil; it tolerates periodic waterlogging and moderate drought once established; prune after flowering to maintain shape and encourage dense growth; harvest leaves in late summer to early fall when terpinen-4-ol content is highest. Tea Tree is the world's most commercially important medicinal Melaleuca — grown on a large scale in the coastal plains of northeastern New South Wales, Australia, where its essential oil is steam-distilled from the leaves and young stems. Tea tree oil is one of the most thoroughly researched of all plant essential oils, with well-documented antibacterial (effective against MRSA and other resistant strains), antifungal, and antiviral properties; it is used in skin care products, toothpastes, wound antiseptics, and household cleaning products worldwide. In the garden, it is an attractive small tree or large shrub with papery, peeling whitish bark, fine needle-like leaves, and showy cream-white bottlebrush flower spikes that are excellent for native bees and other pollinators. Tolerates poorly drained and seasonally wet soils better than most ornamentals, making it useful in low-lying areas of Gulf Coast gardens; it performs best in zone 9–10 in the United States. Caution: tea tree oil is toxic if ingested and should not be used internally.
sources: University of Sydney — Melaleuca alternifolia cultivation
